name: Coria, Galisteo, and Torrejoncillo Loop
name_original: Torrejoncillo dede Coria – circular por Galisteo y el río Alagón
description: Cycle 44.6 miles (71.8 km) on this moderate road cycling route from Coria, exploring Galisteo's historic walls and the scenic río Alagón.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Coria, located in Extremadura, Spain, traverse a diverse landscape characterized by mountains, crags, meadows, and riverbanks. The region offers varied topography with rolling hills and panoramic views, particularly along the Alagón River. Cyclists can find routes with varying difficulty levels, from moderate rides through well-paved areas to more challenging ascents. This environment provides a suitable setting for road cycling across different terrains. Best road cycling routes around Coria The most popular road cycling route is Iron Bridge of Coria – Galisteo Walled Town loop from Coria, a 34.2 miles (55.1 km) trail that takes 2 hours 28 minutes to complete. This route predominantly follows well-paved surfaces with moderate ascents. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Coria, Galisteo, and Torrejoncillo Loop, a moderate 44.6 miles (71.8 km) path. This route traverses diverse topography, including rolling hills and views of the Alagón River. Local road cyclists also love the Coria Castle and Cathedral – Castillo de Eljas loop from Coria, a 71.2 miles (114.5 km) trail leading through varied terrain with significant elevation gain, often completed in about 5 hours 52 minutes. Road cycling around Coria is defined by rolling hills, riverbanks, and diverse topography. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Coria are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 150 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Coria's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of road cycling routes can I find around Coria?
A: Coria and its surroundings offer a diverse range of road cycling routes, catering to various skill levels. You'll find routes with rolling hills, scenic vistas, and paths along the Alagón River. The region's landscape includes mountains, crags, meadows, and riverbanks, providing varied topography for your rides.

Q: Are there routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easier ride?
A: Yes, while many routes feature moderate to difficult ascents, there are options for easier rides. The region's diverse topography includes well-paved surfaces and less challenging sections, particularly along riverbanks, allowing for a more relaxed cycling experience.

Q: What kind of terrain and elevation can I expect on Coria's road cycling routes?
A: Road cycling routes around Coria are characterized by rolling hills and varied topography. You can expect moderate ascents on many routes, with some more challenging options featuring significant elevation gain. The terrain often includes well-paved roads that traverse picturesque countryside and river valleys.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Coria?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Coria are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1056367495/coria-galisteo-and-torrejoncillo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coria, Galisteo, and Torrejoncillo Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular circular route that offers diverse scenery.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: You'll encounter several natural features and historical landmarks. The Alagón River is a central feature, with routes often running along its banks. You can also pass by the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5430399" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iron Bridge of Coria&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6047850" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medieval Bridge of Coria&lt;/a&gt;. The region is also known for its biodiversity, offering opportunities for nature observation.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, Coria and its surroundings offer several viewpoints that provide stunning panoramic views of the landscapes. While specific viewpoints may vary by route, the rolling hills and diverse terrain often present opportunities for scenic stops, especially overlooking the Alagón River and the wider countryside.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Coria?
A: The region of Extremadura generally offers favorable conditions for cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn typically provide pleasant temperatures, making them ideal for road cycling. Summers can be hot, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended during those months.

Q: Is it possible to find family-friendly road cycling routes in Coria?
A: While many road cycling routes involve moderate to difficult ascents, some sections along riverbanks or through flatter areas can be suitable for families. It's advisable to check the elevation profile and distance of specific routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31845971" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iron Bridge of Coria – Galisteo Walled Town loop from Coria&lt;/a&gt;, to ensure they match your family's comfort level.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: Many routes pass through or near towns and villages like Galisteo and Torrejoncillo, where you can find local cafes and establishments to stop for refreshments. Coria itself offers various options before and after your ride.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Coria?
A: The road cycling routes in Coria are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, scenic views, and the opportunity to explore the diverse landscapes of Extremadura.

Q: Can I find challenging road cycling routes with significant elevation gain?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20133047" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coria Castle and Cathedral – Castillo de Eljas loop from Coria&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain and cover longer distances, providing a demanding yet rewarding experience through varied terrain.

Q: Are there options for longer road cycling expeditions starting from Coria?
A: Yes, Coria serves as a good starting point for longer expeditions into the wider Extremadura region. While not directly listed as komoot Smarttours in this guide, the area provides access to extensive networks that can lead to multi-day rides, such as sections of the 'Caceres to Lisbon' route or routes towards 'Hurdes-Batuecas'.

Q: Is parking available for cyclists in Coria?
A: Coria, as a town, offers various parking options. You can typically find public parking areas within or on the outskirts of the town, making it convenient to park your vehicle before starting your road cycling adventure.
tips: Coria has been an important historical city since Roman times due to its strategic location on the valleys of the Alagón river. The city has many impressive monuments and heritage buildings such as the 16th-century Cathedral of Santa María de la Asunción and the 15th-century Episcopal Palace.

Torrejoncillo is a small town in the Valle de Alagón region. The typical narrow streets lead to the Plaza Mayor in the center of town, where the Town Hall and the Church of San Andrés Apóstol are the most outstanding monuments. The city is famous for its handicrafts and festivals.

Galisteo is a historic walled city on the Jerte River dating from Roman times and the Muslim occupation of Spain. The wall that still stands today and that surrounds the Old part of the city is something to marvel at.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a moderate road ride. It covers 71.8 kilometers with an elevation gain of 590 meters, making it suitable for cyclists with good fitness levels. While it has undulating terrain and gradual climbs, it's largely on well-paved roads, offering a manageable challenge.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: You can expect primarily well-paved roads throughout the route. The terrain includes undulating sections and gradual climbs, particularly as you navigate the Alagón Valley, but there are no consistently steep ascents.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers several notable points of interest. You'll pass by the historic Coria Castle and Cathedral, the Plaza Mayor of Torrejoncillo, and the remarkable Galisteo Walled Town. Galisteo is particularly special for its unique circular Almohad wall, built with river pebbles, and its historic center with traditional whitewashed houses.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty, 71.8 km distance, and 590 meters of elevation gain, this route is generally recommended for cyclists with good fitness levels. While it's on paved roads, beginners might find the distance and elevation challenging. It's not ideal for those new to road cycling.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Extremadura region, where this route is located, can get very hot in summer. Therefore, the best times to cycle are during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder, and the landscapes are vibrant, offering a more comfortable and enjoyable experience.

faqs.question: Are there any specific viewpoints or scenic spots?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route offers beautiful scenery, especially through the Alagón River Valley with its fertile fields and riverside forests. The historic town of Galisteo, situated on a slight rise, provides excellent views of the Jerte River and the Alagón Valley, with its mosaic of agricultural colors. The unique Almohad wall of Galisteo is also a significant visual highlight.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the route?
faqs.answer: As the route starts and ends in Coria, you can typically find parking within the town. Look for public parking areas near the historic center or the cathedral to conveniently begin your ride. Specific designated trailhead parking for this exact route may not be signposted, but Coria offers general town parking options.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route primarily on public roads and through towns. While there aren't specific restrictions against dogs on public roads, it's generally not suitable for cycling with dogs due to traffic, distance, and the nature of road cycling. If you plan to stop in towns, be mindful of local regulations regarding pets in public spaces.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It follows public roads and passes through towns, so access is free and open to the public.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see along the Alagón River?
faqs.answer: The Alagón River Valley is known for its rich biodiversity, particularly its thriving birdlife. You might spot various species of waterfowl and other birds that inhabit the riverside forests and agricultural fields. The lush environment provides a habitat for diverse fauna.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route briefly intersect with other paths. You will find small portions that overlap with the Mtb Vía de la Plata and the Vía de la Plata hiking - 02 Merida to Salamanca trail. Other minor intersections include Camino De La Ermita and Pista del Canal. Margen Izquierda.
